coredata.dc:title: A novel chitosan/quercetin-based film enables non-enzymatic detection of glucose: Electrochemical and fluorescent behavior
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yuanhao
Wu, Si
Feng, Tao
Yin, Zili
Wu, Shijing
Wang, Daheng
Fang, Wei
Wang, Qingmiao
Wang, Yu
Hu, Ning
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel functional hydrogel film was synthesized using natural polymer chitosan and quercetin as raw materials. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis indicate that phenylboronic acid modified carbon nanodots (PBA-CDs) were successfully prepared via a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The experimental results demonstrate that a dual electrochemical and fluorescence detection of glucose can be realized based on the boronate ester bonding between chitosan/quercetin (Chit0/Quer) films and PBA-CDs. The addition of PBA-CDs hinders the electron transfer between the phenolic hydroxyl groups of quercetin and the redox mediators, and inhibits the intramolecular rotation of quercetin, which leads to a decreased electrical signal and enhanced fluorescence intensity. After the addition of glucose, the peak redox current of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film is restored due to the competitive binding of glucose to the boronate ester, resulting in a dual-input logic circuit for the qualitative detection of glucose. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel decreases with increasing glucose concentration, and the concentration of glucose can be quantitatively detected by detecting the change in fluorescence intensity. Besides, the sensor has better stability and selectivity for biomarkers. Finally, we demonstrate that the electrochemical and fluorescence sensing strategies can be used for the detection of glucose in real beverage samples. In conclusion, based on the redox activity of quercetin and the competitive binding of boronate ester bonds, we explored a new strategy for enzyme-free electrochemical and fluorescence sensing for the detection of glucose, which is expected to be applied in biomedicine, food industry and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glucose detection
Chitosan
Quercetin
Redox
Electrochemical
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film
Fabrication of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film
Detection of glucose by electrochemical characteristics of the Chit/Quer hydrogel film
Detection of glucose by fluorescence characteristics of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film
Detection of glucose in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22204124 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province ( 2022CFB613 ), and the Key Research and Development Program of Hubei Province ( 2021BCD002 ). We also would like to thank Dr. Jinhui Zhou at the Analytical & Testing Center of Wuhan University of Science and Technology for the help on FT-IR analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the materials. (a) TEM images of PBA-CDs. (b) Particle size distribution of PBA-CDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of Quer (0.02 mg/mL), PBA-CDs (0.02 mg/mL), Quer (0.02 mg/mL)/PBA-CDs (0.02 mg/mL) in phosphate buffer solution. (d) Fluorescence Emission Spectrum of Quer (0.02 mg/mL), PBA-CDs (0.02 mg/mL), Quer (0.02 mg/mL)/PBA-CDs (0.02 mg/mL) in phosphate buffer solution with excitation wavelength at 365 nm.

Structure Characterization of the materials. (a) FT-IR spectra of PBA-CDs, Chit0/Quer and Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs. (b) XPS survey of PBA-CDs. (c) XPS C1s scan of PBA-CDs. (d) XPS O1s scan of PBA-CDs. (e) XPS N1s scan of PBA-CDs. (f) XPS B1s scan of PBA-CDs.

Electrochemical experiments. (a) Switchable redox state of querctin. (b) The reaction diagram of three-electrode system immersed in Fc/Ru3+ solution (50 µM Fc and 50 µM Ru3+ in PB) and redox cycle mechanism of quercetin in Fc/Ru3+ solution. (c) Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film in Fc/Ru3+ solution and the influence of scan rates on reduction peak (CVs performed from 2 to 10000 mV/s). (d) The linear correlation analysis of scan rate and reduction peak current at log10 function, respectively.

Electrochemical response after PBA-CDs and glucose treatment. (a) Mechanism diagram of electrochemical detection of glucose. (b) Electrical input of electrochemical experiment. (c) Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) and I-T curves of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film in PB solution, Fc/Ru3+ solution (50 µM Fc,50 µM Ru3+ in PB) and Fc/Ru3+/Glucose solution (50 µM Fc, 50 µM Ru3+, 10 mM Glucose in PB). (d) Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) and I-T curves of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film in PB solution, Fc/Ru3+ solution, Fc/Ru3+/PBA-CDs solution (50 µM Fc, 50 µM Ru3+, 1 mg/mL PBA-CDs in PB) and Fc/Ru3+/ Glucose solution. All scan rate is 50 mV/s.

Electrochemical logic circuit gate. (a) Schematic of electrical sensing strategy for the detection of glucose. (b) Cyclic voltammograms of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film corresponding to the various input signals: (0, 0), (1, 0), (0, 1), (1, 1). (c) Reduction peak current as a function of input signals. (d) Truth table of the various input signals. (e) NOT and OR logic circuit gate.

Fluorescence detection of glucose. (a) Experimental process and schematic diagram of glucose fluorescence detection mechanism. (b) Fluorescence intensity changes (ΔFL Intensity) of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel films before and after treatment with 5 mM glucose solution for 30 min. (c) Relationship between ΔFL Intensity and glucose concentration.
Fluorescence stability experiments of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film. (a) The effect of temperature. (b) The effect of time.
Glucose detection in real samples. (a) Electrochemical detection of soda and diet soda. (b) Fluorescence detection of soda and diet soda.

head.ce:title: A novel chitosan/quercetin-based film enables non-enzymatic detection of glucose: Electrochemical and fluorescent behavior

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources and Environment Engineering
Wuhan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel functional hydrogel film was synthesized using natural polymer chitosan and quercetin as raw materials. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis indicate that phenylboronic acid modified carbon nanodots (PBA-CDs) were successfully prepared via a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The experimental results demonstrate that a dual electrochemical and fluorescence detection of glucose can be realized based on the boronate ester bonding between chitosan/quercetin (Chit0/Quer) films and PBA-CDs. The addition of PBA-CDs hinders the electron transfer between the phenolic hydroxyl groups of quercetin and the redox mediators, and inhibits the intramolecular rotation of quercetin, which leads to a decreased electrical signal and enhanced fluorescence intensity. After the addition of glucose, the peak redox current of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film is restored due to the competitive binding of glucose to the boronate ester, resulting in a dual-input logic circuit for the qualitative detection of glucose. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel decreases with increasing glucose concentration, and the concentration of glucose can be quantitatively detected by detecting the change in fluorescence intensity. Besides, the sensor has better stability and selectivity for biomarkers. Finally, we demonstrate that the electrochemical and fluorescence sensing strategies can be used for the detection of glucose in real beverage samples. In conclusion, based on the redox activity of quercetin and the competitive binding of boronate ester bonds, we explored a new strategy for enzyme-free electrochemical and fluorescence sensing for the detection of glucose, which is expected to be applied in biomedicine, food industry and other fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose detection is of great importance in the food industry, healthcare, biomedical and other fields[1–3]. For example, diabetic patients need to closely monitor their blood glucose levels to avoid serious complications[4]. Currently, enzyme-based biosensors are widely used in rapid detection due to their high specificity and strong adaptability[5]. Usually, the sensors have the functions of molecular recognition, signal transformation and signal amplification. Glucose is recognized and catalyzed by glucose oxidase to produce hydrogen peroxide, which is able to be converted into signals such as chemiluminescence, fluorescence, electrochemical current, etc., and the glucose content can be determined through signal amplification and data processing[6–14]. However, modification of biosensors by enzymes is usually complicated and expensive, and the enzymes are easily denatured and inactivated. Therefore, it is critical to develop stable and efficient enzyme-free sensors for the rapid and accurate detection of glucose.

Research on enzyme-free sensors for glucose has focused on catalysis by conducting polymers (noble metals, transition metals, metal oxides, metal–organic framework materials, etc.) as well as functionalized molecular modifications to introduce recognition sites for glucose, converting the detected signals into measurable electrochemical redox currents, optical, fluorescent signals, and so on[15,16]. Precious metals have high catalytic activity for glucose oxidation, but their limited storage and high cost hinder their practical application. In recent years, the assembly of specific functionalized molecules on sensors that can selectively recognize multiple targets has been widely used in the development of non-enzymatic sensors[17]. Based on the interaction of boronate ester bond with glucose, scholars have developed various types of non-enzymatic glucose sensors[18]. It has been demonstrated that borate-functionalized carbon nanodots form a rigid structure with quercetin through boronate ester and hydrogen bonding, which inhibits the intramolecular rotation of quercetin, resulting in a strong fluorescence response[19]. The boronic acid moiety binds to glucose with greater affinity than the phenolic hydroxyl group. Consequently, the introduction of phenylboronic acid into the phenolic moiety to form a dynamic boronate ester not only enhances the hydrogel strength and antioxidant capacity[20], but also amplifies the electrical signal of glucose detection[21].

Quercetin (3,3′,4′,5,7-pentahydroxyflavone) is a widespread flavonoid in daily life, which mainly comes from fruits and vegetables, such as apples, oranges, onions, garlic, onions, black tea, celery, kale, blueberries and so on[22–25]. It has been proved that flavonoid quercetin and other related polyphenols play a certain role in the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases, cardiovascular diseases, cancer and other diseases[26]. However, quercetin is hydrophobic, making it difficult to disperse in biopolymer matrices. Adsorption of quercetin onto hydrophilic nanoparticles (e.g., chitosan nanoparticles) is an effective approach to improve the dispersibility of quercetin[27,28]. Chitosan and quercetin polysaccharides have been demonstrated to have good application potential in biomedical and other safety sensitive fields[29–31]. Chitosan is a natural cationic polymer, which mainly exists in the cell walls of fungi, the shells of crustaceans and the cuticle of insects. Chitosan is non-toxic, anti-bacteria, biocompatible, and easy to be chemically modified, offering a super extensive applications in the fields of intelligent packaging materials, drug delivery, biosensors and other fields[32–34].

Here, an enzyme-free sensor was constructed using biodegradable chitosan and redox-active quercetin as substrate materials. Phenylboronic acid modified carbon nanodots (PBA-CDs) were assembled with quercetin through the interaction of boronate ester and hydrogen bonding. Based on the intramolecular rotation of quercetin and the selective molecular recognition mechanism of phenylboronic acid on glucose, we achieved fluorescence and electrical signal responses for glucose detection.

Chitosan (200–600 mPa.s) was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry (TCI, Shanghai). D-(+)-Glucose was acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Quercetin hydrate was acquired from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (4-Chlorocarbonylphenyl)Boronic Anhydride (CFPBA) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ethylenediamine (EDA) was obtained from Tianjin New Technology Industrial Park Kemao Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Phosphoric acid, Sodium hydrogen carbonate, Sodium hydroxide, Ethanol absolute, Acetonitrile were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hexaammineruthenium(III) chloride (98 %, Ru3+)and 1,1′Ferrocenedimethanol (98 %, Fc) were purchased from Acros Organics. All reagents were used as received without further purification. The ultra-pure water used in this study was obtained from a Super-Q water system (Pinguan).

Preparation of N-Cds: Firstly, 3 mL of EDA (Ethylenediamine) was dissolved in 18 mL of ultra-pure water. Next, 6 mL of phosphoric acid was dripped and stirred to form a homogeneous solution. The mixture was transferred to a beaker, heated in a microwave digestion apparatus for 3 min, and then cooled, resulting in a dark brown gelatinous solid. The solid was fully dissolved by adding 75 mL of ultra-pure water, and the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 7 with sodium hydroxide solution. After that, the mixture was filtered by 0.22 mm filter paper to remove large or aggregated particles. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed (MWCO: 500dA) for 12 h, then freeze-dried to obtain the yellowish N-CDs powder.

Preparation of PBA-CDs: The N-CDs (71.92 mg) were dissolved in 1.81 mL ultra-pure water. Then, 90.58 mg sodium bicarbonate was added to this solution. The mixed solution was cooled in an ice water bath for 30 min. Next, 500 mg CFPBA was added to 4 mL acetonitrile, kept suspended and continuously ventilated with nitrogen in an ice water bath. The two solutions were fully mixed and placed at room temperature for 2 h. The acetonitrile was then removed by rotary evaporation for 2 h. Finally, the resulting mixture was then dialyzed and freeze-dried to obtain the PBA-CDs power.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film
ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin solution (3 mg/mL) was prepared with ethanol solution as solvent. Chitosan solution was prepared by dissolving chitosan (10 mg/mL) with stoichiometric amounts of hydrochloric acid (final pH 5.5). The same volume of quercetin solution was mixed with chitosan solution to form a Chit-H+/Quer blend solution (5 mg/mL chitosan, 1.5 mg/mL quercetin).

The Chit-H+/Quer solution (about 0.3 g) was dropped on the surface of the standard gold electrode, and dried for 20 min, and then treated with PB solution (phosphate buffer; 0.1 M; pH 7.0) for 15 min. During this process, the protonated chitosan (Chit-H+) was neutralized to form chitosan hydrogel network (Chit0), resulting in the formation of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film. The prepared Chit0/Quer hydrogel film was utilized for the electrochemical detection of glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film
ce:section.ce:para: PBA-CDs were dissolved in the Chit-H+/Quer solution to form a Chit-H+/Quer/PBA-CDs blend solution (5 mg/mL chitosan, 1.5 mg/mL quercetin, 1 mg/mL PBA-CDs). Then the Chit-H+/Quer/PBA-CDs solution was poured on a JGS1 quartz glass evenly, dried for 20 min, and then treated with PB solution for 25 min. This light yellow film obtained was designated as Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film, which was applied for the fluorescence detection of glucose.

Electrochemical experiments were performed using a three-electrode system with a standard gold electrode (2 mm diameter) as a working electrode, a platinum wire counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The Chit0/Quer hydrogel film modified gold electrode was separately immersed in PB solution, Fc/Ru3+ solution (50 µM Fc and 50 µM Ru3+ in PB), Fc/Ru3+/PBA-CDs solution and Fc/Ru3+/glucose solution to measure the redox signals.

The Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel films were treated with 0, 0.1, 1, 5 and 10 mM glucose solution for 30 min, respectively. After these hydrogel films were dried in an oven, the fluorescence emission spectra of Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel films were measured before and after glucose treatment using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 399 nm.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2550, SHIMADZU, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (INVENIO R, BRUKER, Germany). The formation of PBA-CDs was measured using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Nexsa, Thermo SCIENTIFIC, America). The morphology of PBA-CDs was obtained by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan). The fluorescence of Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel films were detected using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000, SHIMADZU, Japan).

The morphology and particle size of the prepared PBA-CDs were first studied. PBA-CDs was dispersed in pure aqueous solution and tested by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). The TEM image in Fig. 1 a shows that the particle size of PBA-CDs is about 2.2 nm, and the insert image exhibits a graphite lattice (100) distance of 0.21 nm. Fig. 1b illustrates the particle size distribution of PBA-CDs.

To explore the effect of PBA-CDs on the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of quercetin (Quer) solution, UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence experiments were performed, as shown in Fig. 1c and d. Experimentally, Quer (0.02 mg/mL), PBA-CDs (0.02 mg/mL), Quer (0.02 mg/mL)/PBA-CDs (0.02 mg/mL) in phosphate buffer (PB) solution were tested by UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra, respectively. The UV–Vis spectra in Fig. 1c shows that Quer solution have two absorption bands at 260 nm and 365 nm. While the peak adsorbance of Quer (0.02 mg/mL)/PBA-CDs (0.02 mg/mL) solution increased at 365 nm. Then, 365 nm is used as the excitation wavelength for fluorescence emission spectra.

Fig. 1d demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of quercetin is eight-fold enhanced after the addition of PBA-CDs, which proves that there is some kind of intermolecular interaction between quercetin and PBA-CDs. The insert in Fig. 1d shows the structural formula of quercetin. The single bond between C1′ of the B-ring and C2 of the C-ring implies that there may exists an intramolecular rotational vibration, which are likely to lead to additional excitation energy depletion[19]. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of quercetin solution is weak. Since the formation of boronate ester bond between the PBA-CDs and the 1,2-diols of quercetin inhibits the intramolecular rotation of quercetin, resulting in an enhanced fluorescence intensity upon the addition of PBA-CDs. The disappearance of the fluorescence peak of PBA-CDs is due to the static fluorescence mechanism caused by the boronate ester bond induced by PBA-CDs and quercetin. Ao et al. revealed that the addition of p-Nitrophenol was able to quench the fluorescence of phenylboronic acid functionalized carbon quantum dot (PBA-CDQ) through a static quenching mechanism, which was attributed to the formation of B-O bond between them[35].

To further determine the interaction mechanism between Quer and PBA-CDs, the Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of PBA-CDs, Chit0/Quer and Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs were demonstrated in Fig. 2 a. For PBA-CDs, the strong absorption bands at 3393 and 3174 cm−1 belong to stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H[36]. The peaks at 1692 and 1560 cm−1 are owing to the C=O stretching of acetyl (amide I) and the stretching and bending vibration of N–H (amide II), respectively[37]. The peak at 1354 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of B–O in PBA-CDs. The peak at 1272 cm−1 is attributed to the B-O-H bending vibration[38]. These results indicate that there are a lot of various functional groups on the surface of PBA-CDS, such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups[39]. For Chit0/Quer, the absorption peak at 3425 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2 bands, the peak at 1083 cm−1 stem from stretching vibration of C-O[40], the peak at 2937 cm−1 is due to the C–H stretching vibration of the alkane group of chitosan[41]. For Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs, the peaks of Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs are similar to those of Chit0/Quer and PBA-CDs, suggesting the existence of intermolecular interactions between quercetin and PBA-CDs. The peak at 3425 cm−1 of Chit0/Quer decreases to 3417 cm−1 in the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs, indicating that PBA-CDs interact with the hydroxyl of quercetin to form boronate ester bonds and hydrogen bonds[42,43].

To further analyze the possible components or bonding forms of the PBA-CDs, the XPS survey and high-resolution XPS spectra of the PBA-CDs were fitted with the related elements. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peaks of 193.9, 284.9, 401.7, 532.7 eV represent B1s, C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively, indicating that PBA was successfully loaded on the CDs. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) of PBA-CDs can be deconvoluted into peaks at 284.8, 285.7, 286.5, 288.7, 290.4 eV, which are assignable to CC/CH, CN, CO, CO, π − π*, respectively[44]. The O1s band (Fig. 2d) has two peaks located at 532.3 eV and 533.9 eV, representing the CO, COC/COH group[45]. The high resolution N1s band in Fig. 2e reveals pyrrolic N (399.4 eV), amide nitrogen atoms (400.2 eV), graphitic N (402.0 eV) and Oxidized N (404.1 eV)[46]. Fig. 2f illustrates that the signals of BO bond in B2O3 and BC bond appear at 193.64 eV and 191.3 eV[47]. These results indicate the PBA-CDs were successfully synthesized by a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose by electrochemical characteristics of the Chit/Quer hydrogel film
ce:section.ce:para: The redox activity of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film was first studied. Previous studies have shown that catechol has redox activity[48,49]. Fig. 3 a illustrates that catechol has two stable redox states (reduced catechol and oxidized quinone) and one unstable intermediate state (semiquinone)[50]. Since quercetin also has a catechol moiety, the redox activity is similar to that of catechol. As shown in Fig. 3b, the ability of quercetin to gain and lose electrons allows it to participate in redox media-mediated redox cycling processes. In the reduction cycles, Ru3+ converts oxidized o-quinone (Q) of quercetin into reduced catechol moiety (QH2), whereas in the oxidation cycles, Fc transforms reduced catechol moiety of quercetin into their oxidized o-quinone, thus the electron transfer between the electrode and the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film is regulated.

The dynamic response of electrochemical outputs was next performed to explore the relationship between oxidation potential and scan rate. Experimentally, the Chit-H+/Quer solution was added dropwise to the surface of a standard gold electrode, dried, and subsequently neutralized with PB solution to obtain a neutralized Chit0/Quer hydrogel film (The deprotonated state of chitosan was designated as Chit0). Then, the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film modified electrode was immersed in Fc/Ru3+ solution (50 μM Fc and 50 μM Ru3+ in PB). And the electrochemical workstation was used to measure the CVs of the electrode at various scan rates (2 to 10000 mV/s). Fig. 3c shows that the electrochemical current increased with scan rates. The dynamic analysis of reduction peak current changes was further displayed in Fig. 3d, which reveals a good linear relationship. The Eqs. (1) and (2) below show power-law dependencies, where the exponent (b) provides mechanistic insight: b close to 1 indicates a surface-controlled electrochemical process, and b close to 0.5 indicates a diffusion-controlled process[51,52].
(1)
i
V
= a ·
v
b
(2)
l o g
i
V
= b · l o g
v
+ l o g
a

Where
i
V
represents the peak current, a stand for the coefficient, b denotes the exponent.

In the presence of mediators, the exponent for the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film (b = 0.496) supports the assumption that mediator diffusion is essential for electron-transfer with non-conducting quercetin[53–55].

To explore the ability of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film to detect glucose, electrochemical experiments were investigated. The schematic in Fig. 4 a shows the reaction mechanism. The electron transfer between the redox mediators (Fc and Ru3+) and quercetin is inhibited due to the formation of boronate esters between the boronic acid groups of PBA-CDs and the phenolic hydroxyl groups of quercetin. After the addition of glucose, the redox signal of quercetin can be restored due to the competitive binding of the 1,2-diols of glucose to PBA-CDs.

Fig. 4b demonstrates the electrical input signal. The electrical outputs were measured after glucose treatment in Fig. 4c. Experimentally, the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film modified gold electrode was put into PB solution, Fc/Ru3+ solution (50 μM Fc and 50 μM Ru3+ in PB) and Fc/Ru3+/Glucose solution (50 µM Fc, 50 µM Ru3+, 10 mM Glucose in PB), respectively. The top curve in Fig. 4c reveals that the redox signals is comparatively low in PB solution. While amplified redox peaks can be observed in Fc/Ru3+ solution, which is attributed to the phenolic hydroxyl group of quercetin. The incubation of glucose essentially does not change the redox peak current values. The curves below are the last three CV cycles, which represent that the redox signals are very stable. These results demonstrate that the existence of glucose will not directly affect the redox cycle of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film.

As shown in Fig. 4d, another experiment was performed to prove the possibility of glucose detection by PBA-CDs treated hydrogels. Likewise, the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film modified gold electrode was put into PB solution, Fc/Ru3+ solution (50 µM Fc, 50 µM Ru3+ in PB), Fc/Ru3+/PBA-CDs solution (50 µM Fc, 50 µM Ru3+, 1 mg/mL PBA-CDs in PB), Fc/Ru3+/ Glucose solution (50 µM Fc, 50 µM Ru3+, 10 mM Glucose in PB), respectively. Three parallel tests were performed for each group during the experiment. It can be seen that the addition of PBA-CDs impedes the electron transfer between the redox mediators (Fc and Ru3+) and quercetin, resulting in a reduced current signal. And the redox signals can be recovered after adding glucose, because the competitive binding of the 1,2-diols bond of glucose to PBA-CDs disrupts the binding of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film and PBA-CDs, thus reactivating the redox cycles. Therefore, the presence of glucose can be detected according to the change of redox peak current before and after the addition of glucose.

The electrochemical sensing strategy allows the detection of glucose via a two-input IMP logic circuit gate. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the equivalent circuit defines PBA-CDs as input A and glucose as input B. In the logic circuit, the mediator solution (Fc/Ru3+) containing PBA-CDs or glucose is considered as input “1″. The solution without these two substances is considered to be input “0”. Reduction peak current (redox cycling of Ru3+) of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film modified electrode in mediator solution is regarded as output “1”. And reduction peak current inhibition (>20 %) is defined as output “0”. Based on the above definition, the CVs measured in different solutions (input signals), reduction peak currents (output signals) and corresponding truth table are shown in Fig. 5b–d, respectively. When the CVs was running in mediator solutions (input (0,0), without PBA-CDs and glucose), the reduction peak current of observed as 3.97 µA (output 1). After adding PBA-CDs in mediator solutions (input (1,0)), the reduction peak current decreased to 2.91 µA (output 0), due to the blocking of electron transfer by the boronate ester formed by PBA-CDs and quercetin. The addition of only glucose in mediator solution does not affect the redox signals (input (0, 1), output 1). However, when the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film modified electrode was first treated with a mediator solution containing PBA-CDs and then swept CVs in a glucose-containing mediator solution (input (1,1)), the reduction peak current increased to 3.85 µA. As a result of the competition of glucose, the boronate ester between PBA-CDs and quercetin breaks, resulting in the recovering of electron transfer between quercetin and the mediators. Fig. 5e demonstrates the equivalent circuit of a two-input IMP logic gate. Based on these analyses, we confirm that the electrochemical detection of Chit0/Quer hydrogel film can determine the existence of glucose.

To further demonstrate the selectivity of electrochemical detection, the CV curves of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film in various substance solutions (Uric acid, L-Cysteine, glutathione, ascorbic acid and fructose) were measured in Fig. S1. The results indicate that the electrochemical sensing strategy is not selective for fructose, which can also form boronate esters with PBA-CDs, but has selectivity for other biomarkers (Uric acid, L-Cysteine, glutathione and ascorbic acid).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose by fluorescence characteristics of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film
ce:section.ce:para: To further verify the possibility of quercetin fluorescence sensing strategy for quantitative detection of glucose, fluorescence experiments was performed. Fig. 6 a illustrates the experimental process and mechanism of glucose detection. A mixed solution of PBA-CDs, chitosan and quercetin is added to the surface of a JGS1 quartz glass to form a Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film. Then, the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film was immersed in various concentration of glucose solution (0,0.1, 1, 5 and 10 mM) for 30 min, three parallel tests were performed for each group during the experiment. Fig. 6b shows that the fluorescence intensity of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film is weak, while the fluorescence intensity of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film increases a lot. The reason is that PBA-CDs immobilize the 1,2-diols structure of quercetin and inhibit the internal rotation of quercetin, thus enhancing the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel film. The fluorescence intensity decreases significantly after 10 mM glucose treatment. Since the 1,2-diols of glucose binds competitively to PBA-CDs, thereby destroying the boronate esters formed by quercetin and PBA-CDs. The fluorescence difference (ΔFL Intensity) was calculated before and after treatment with different concentrations of glucose solution. Fig. 6c demonstrates a linear relationship between ΔFL Intensity and the concentration of glucose solution, which makes it possible to detect glucose quantitatively by fluorescence sensing strategy in the rage of 0.1 to 10 mM. The detection limit (LOD=3SD/b) of fluorescence detection is calculated as 2.15 mM. SD represents the standard deviation of the blank value and b is the slope of the linear regression equation.

The selectivity of fluorescence detection was next examined. In Fig. S2, fluorescence tests were performed by immersing the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film in various substance solutions (Uric acid, L-Cysteine, glutathione, ascorbic acid and fructose), and measuring the fluorescence intensity. The results demonstrate that the fluorescence sensing strategy has selectivity for uric acid, L-Cysteine, glutathione and ascorbic acid, except for fructose.

The stability of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film was measured by both storage temperature and storage time. The effect of temperature was first discussed. Experimentally, the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel films were treated at 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 and 80 °C, and the florescence intensity at 564 nm was recorded. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the florescence intensity is relatively stable from 30 to 70°C, with a slight decrease at 80°C. To investigate the effect of storage time, the florescence intensity of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film was measured daily for ten consecutive days. The results in Fig. 7b demonstrate that there is no significant change in florescence intensity, further proving the feasibility to detect glucose by using this fluorescence sensing strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The above results indicate that glucose can be detected qualitatively by an electrochemical sensing strategy and quantitatively by a fluorescence sensing strategy. To demonstrate the capability of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film for glucose detection in real beverages, we tested commonly consumed soda and diet soda beverages. For electrochemical detection, the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film modified electrode was first treated with a mediator solution containing PBA-CDs (50 µM Fc, 50 µM Ru3+, 1 mg/mL PBA-CDs), and then immersed in a mediator solution containing either soda or diet soda (the beverages were diluted tenfold), the CV curves were recorded in Fig. S3a. Fig. 8 a shows that after soda treatment, the reduction peak current of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film increases to 3.37 µA. However, there is no significant change after diet soda treatment. This indicates that we have successfully detected glucose in soda, while glucose cannot be detected in diet soda. The results suggest that it is possible to distinguish the presence of glucose in beverages by an electrochemical sensing strategy.

For fluorescence detection, the fluorescence intensity of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film before and after treatment with soda or diet soda beverages was measured in Fig. S3b. Fig. 8b illustrates the fluorescence intensity changes (ΔFL intensity) of the Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film. Based on the formula in Fig. 6c, it can be concluded that the concentration of glucose contained in soda is 36.2 mM, which is very close to the result of 10 g/100 mL in real samples, while no glucose is present in diet soda. These results indicate that the concentration of glucose in beverages can be detected by a fluorescence sensing strategy.

In this study, we synthesized a novel functional Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film by using PBA-CDs that were prepared by a simple microwave-assisted hydrothermal method and environmentally friendly and biocompatible bio-derived materials (quercetin and chitosan). The hydrogel film exhibits good stability and selectivity for biomarkers and can detect glucose utilizing a dual electrochemical and fluorescence sensing strategy. The addition of PBA-CDs impedes the electron transfer between the catechol and the mediators, and the competitive binding of glucose to the boronate ester restores the peak redox current of the Chit0/Quer hydrogel film, resulting in a two-input logic circuit for the qualitative detection of glucose. Furthermore, due to the special intermolecular interaction between the ortho-hydroxyl groups of quercetin and phenylboric acid, quercetin and PBA-CDs inhibit the intramolecular rotation and lead to the enhancement of fluorescence, while the presence of glucose destroys the boronate ester, leading to the decrease of fluorescence intensity. Thus, the concentration of glucose can be quantitatively detected by detecting the change of fluorescence intensity. Finally, we demonstrate that the electrochemical and fluorescence sensing strategies can be used for the detection of glucose in real beverage samples.

In conclusion, we prepared a simple and environmentally friendly Chit0/Quer/PBA-CDs hydrogel film, and explored new electrochemical and fluorescence strategies of enzyme-free sensing for glucose detection, which is expected to be applied in biomedical and other fields. Moreover, this work further illustrates the potential of quercetin-based materials with redox functionality for sensing applications. Although this study may have some shortcomings, for instance, the detection limits and dynamic ranges of the quantitative assay need to be improved, and the influence of other factors (e.g., pH, ions) on the assay remains to be explored. We expect to further refine the sensing strategy in the future and explore a simpler, more sensitive, highly selective and environmentally friendly glucose detection strategy.

Yuanhao Yan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Si Wu: Investigation, Validation. Tao Feng: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Zili Yin: Formal analysis, Investigation. Shijing Wu: Investigation, Validation. Daheng Wang: Resources, Methodology. Wei Fang: Resources, Investigation. Qingmiao Wang: Formal analysis, Software. Yu Wang: Validation, Resources. Ning Hu: Methodology. Li Wang: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22204124), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2022CFB613), and the Key Research and Development Program of Hubei Province (2021BCD002). We also would like to thank Dr. Jinhui Zhou at the Analytical & Testing Center of Wuhan University of Science and Technology for the help on FT-IR analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111653.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
